title: Matsvinskollen
subtitle: A quiz-based mobile prototype exploring whether gamification can shift how people think about everyday food decisions.

intro.heading: Not a competition, a way to become a food hero
intro.body:
Matsvinskollen started with a research question: why do people waste food even when they know they shouldn't? What I found was that food waste in schools is often driven by behaviour patterns and cultural norms around food, not lack of awareness.

The course focused on gamification, so the idea of a lighthearted quiz became the starting point. Not a competition about who knows the most, but a way to become a food hero.

role.heading: My role
role.body: I worked across research, interaction design, wireframing, and prototyping. My main contribution was defining how the experience should feel and making sure that feeling came through in the structure of the prototype, not just the visuals.

problem.heading: Defining what should actually be built
problem.body: The challenge was not just picking a topic, it was finding something specific enough to actually test within three weeks. Food waste is broad. We had to narrow it down to one interaction idea that could realistically show whether gamification could shift how people think about everyday food decisions.
bullets: Defining a focused concept that could realistically be explored within a three-week project
Turning abstract food waste knowledge into simple, interactive learning moments
Designing a quiz interaction that feels engaging without becoming overly complex
Communicating feedback clearly so users immediately understand the impact of their choices

approach.heading: Designing the quiz interaction
approach.body:
I started with wireframes to map the flow before touching visuals. One early decision was whether to use photos of real food or illustrations. We tested both, and illustrations won clearly as they were easier to read quickly and felt more consistent with the lighthearted tone we were going for.

I structured the prototype as reusable components in Figma so we could iterate without rebuilding screens each time.

image.alt: Wireframe of the Matsvinskollen quiz flow

interactionSystem.heading: Designing the interaction system
interactionSystem.body:
Because the experience revolves around answering questions, clear feedback was essential.

I designed reusable UI components that communicate different interaction states, including default selection, correct answers, and incorrect answers.

Structuring these states as components made the prototype easier to maintain and ensured the interaction stayed consistent throughout the experience.

image.alt: Component-based interaction states designed in Figma

outcome.heading: The tone worked
outcome.body:
What stood out during testing was not how many questions people got right, it was how relaxed they felt doing it. The tone worked. People engaged with the quiz as something fun rather than a test.

It was also interesting that questions which seemed straightforward, like how long milk actually lasts, caught almost everyone out, including me. That gap between assumed knowledge and actual knowledge was exactly what we were trying to surface.

image.alt: Prototype flow of the Matsvinskollen quiz experience

keyLearning.heading: One level was enough to learn from
keyLearning.body:
I would have prioritised an English version earlier, both to reach a wider audience and to challenge the copywriting. A lot of what made the quiz work was the tone of the questions, and translating that into another language is its own design problem.

We also only completed one level. The prototype ends with a short film teasing what comes next. It was intentional, we were testing one idea, not building a product, but finishing it fully would have given a much clearer picture of the whole experience.

cards.title: Structure before detail
cards.body: Starting with wireframes and flow mapping helped define how users move through the quiz before focusing on visual design.

cards.title: Test the tone, not just the function
cards.body: The most valuable insight from testing was not whether the interaction worked, it was whether people felt the way we intended. Tone is a design decision too.
